title: Phase-tunable remote nonreciprocal charging in waveguide QED
authors: Meixi Guo, Jian Huang, Rui-Yang Gong, Xian-Li Yin, Guofeng Zhang
abstract: Remote quantum batteries require directional and controllable energy transfer between spatially separated quantum nodes, yet most existing protocols rely on direct charger-battery Hamiltonian couplings. Here we propose a phase-tunable waveguide-QED architecture for remote quantum-battery charging, in which a driven charger and a remote battery are coupled solely via engineered waveguide-mediated interference, without any direct local interaction. We systematically compare four configurations: two-giant-emitter and giant-small-emitter hybrids, each with open or mirror-terminated waveguides. By engineering the propagation and coupling phases, the waveguide-mediated coherent exchange interaction and collective dissipation can be balanced to suppress the backward channel while retaining a finite forward channel, thereby realizing cascaded-like unidirectional charging. Our analysis shows that nonreciprocity and storage efficiency can be independently engineered, offering design flexibility for different quantum network scenarios. The giant-small-emitter mirror-terminated configuration simultaneously achieves perfect nonreciprocity and battery-dominated storage, while both giant-small-emitter configurations exhibit distance-insensitive directionality. Extending the scheme to quadratic driving, we show that anomalous second moments render the battery state non-passive, making ergotropy a performance metric distinct from stored energy. These results establish phase-tunable waveguide networks as a versatile platform for remote quantum-energy transfer and provide design principles for directional and work-extractable energy storage in quantum networks.
summary: This paper proposes a method to charge remote quantum batteries using waveguide-mediated interference instead of direct coupling. By tuning propagation and coupling phases, the researchers show how to achieve unidirectional, nonreciprocal energy flow. The study also reveals that quadratic driving can create non-passive battery states where work extractability (ergotropy) becomes a distinct metric from stored energy.
main_problem: Developing efficient protocols for remote quantum battery charging where the charger and battery are spatially separated, specifically overcoming the need for direct local Hamiltonian couplings and achieving unidirectional energy transfer.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that phase-tunable waveguide-QED architectures can realize nonreciprocal charging, with the 3P+M configuration achieving both perfect nonreciprocity and battery-dominated storage, while 3P setups offer distance-insensitive directionality.
method: The study uses the SLH formalism to derive a quantum master equation and analyzes the time evolution of first and second moments under linear and quadratic driving regimes.
model_or_system: A waveguide QED architecture consisting of two single-mode cavities (a driven charger and a remote battery) coupled to a 1D waveguide, exploring four configurations: 4P, 4P+M, 3P, and 3P+M (open and mirror-terminated).
key_observables: Nonreciprocal ratio (R), relative storage ratio (eta), stored energy (Eb), ergotropy (W), and anomalous second moments.
important_parameters: Propagation phases (phi_w), coupling phases (theta), waveguide coupling rate (gamma), driving amplitude (Omega), and cavity decay rates (kappa).
main_assumptions: The system assumes a waveguide-mediated regime with no direct local interaction, negligible retardation effects, and small frequency mismatch between modes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows schematic illustrations of the four waveguide-QED configurations; Figure 2 shows time evolution of scaled energies; Figure 4 shows steady-state relative storage ratios.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of remote charging, describes the waveguide-QED models and configurations, provides analytical derivations for the master equation coefficients, compares the performance of the four setups, and extends the analysis to quadratic driving and ergotropy.
why_interesting: It provides a versatile design principle for directional energy transfer in quantum networks using interference and reservoir engineering, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of four different waveguide- mediated nonreciprocal QB configurations: (a) Open four- point model (4P), (b) Mirror-terminated four-point model (4P+M), (c) Open three-point model (3P), and (d) Mirror- terminated three-point model (3P+M). Each setup consists of two single-mode cavities (a driven charger and a remote QB) coupled to a one-dimensional waveguide. In the four-point designs (a,b), both cavity modes are coupled to the waveg- uide at two spatially separated points with waveguide cou- pling rates γ11, γ12, γ21, and γ22. In the three-point designs (c,d), the left cavity mode is configured as a small emitter and couples to the waveguide at a single point...

FIG. 2. Nonreciprocal charging dynamics for different se- tups: (a) 4P, (b) 4P+M, (c) 3P, and (d) 3P+M. Curves show the time evolution of the scaled energies Eb/ωb and Ec/ωc under left- and right-mode driving. Here, ωb and ωc denote the frequencies of the QB and charger modes, respectively: under left-mode driving, ωb = ω2 and ωc = ω1, while under right-mode driving, ωb = ω1 and ωc = ω2. Red and yellow triangles denote the remote battery and driven-mode ener- gies under left-mode driving, EL,nr b and EL,nr c , respectively. Green and blue squares show the corresponding energies un- der right-mode driving; here ER,nr b denotes the energy stored in the remote receiver (the left mode a1),...

FIG. 3. Contour maps of the steady-state nonreciprocal ra- tio R(∞) as a function of the QB coupling phase θ2 and the propagation phase ϕw. The white markers indicate parame- ter values for which R(∞) = 1, while the cyan-blue markers indicate parameter values for which R(∞) = −1. In panels (a) and (b), these conditions are marked by dots; in panels (c) and (d), they are marked by vertical lines. The setups cor- respond to (a) 4P, (b) 4P+M, (c) 3P, and (d) 3P+M. Other parameters used are the same as those in Fig. 2.

FIG. 4. Relative storage ratio under left-mode driving. Color maps show the relative storage ratio η(∞) as a func- tion of phase θ2 and ϕw for (a) 4P, (b) 4P+M, (c) 3P, and (d) 3P+M. The white contour marks η = 1, separating battery- dominated storage (η > 1) from charger-dominated storage (η < 1). Other parameters used are the same as those in Fig. 2.

FIG. 6. Extractable fraction of the stored energy under quadratic driving. Curves show the ratio ζL,nr b (t) as a function of γt for the four configurations: 4P, 4P+M, 3P, and 3P+M. Other parameters used are the same as those in Fig. 5.

Partially Pumped Collective Emitters.note: The paper directly studies a configuration where a specific 'charger' cavity is driven while a remote 'battery' cavity remains passive, focusing on the excitation transfer and energy storage dynamics between these differently driven elements.
